summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the trend of blogs cluttered with excessive widgets, icons, and navigation complexity. Using the 'flair' metaphor from Office Space, he points to examples of blogs with dozens of social bookmarking icons, multiple RSS subscription buttons, and confusing navigation that obscures content. He argues that blogs succeed because of their simplicity, and that adding visual noise makes them harder to read and navigate. The post concludes by suggesting RSS feeds as a solution that delivers content without the clutter.
key_points: Social bookmarking icons are useless visual noise - power users already know how to bookmark, and everyone else is confused by them
Blogs work because they're simple; adding flair undermines their core strength
Poor navigation choices (click-to-continue, buried browse links, limited archives) actively harm user experience
Statistics and widgets that interest the blogger are just noise to readers
RSS represents the ideal: content delivery without visual clutter
money_quotes: Why do people want their blogs to look like NASCAR vehicles?
Blogs work because they're simple. Adding a bunch of flair just makes them harder to navigate and more difficult to read.
The users of the above social bookmarking sites surely know how to bookmark a site without these 'helpful' icons. Everyone else is befuddled by 24 meaningless icons.
I'd love to browse the rest of Tom's entries, but he's made it awfully difficult for me to do so.
Perhaps this is really an argument in favor of RSS – all of the content with none of the excess flair.
I'm sure Scott finds them interesting, but they're just noise to me.
key_insight: Every widget, icon, and feature you add to a blog should be evaluated from the reader's perspective, not the author's - most 'helpful' additions are just noise that obscures the content people came for.
